items.q: What produces a magnetic field around a wire?
items.correct: A moving electric charge — i.e. an electric current.
items.misconception: The metal in the wire is naturally magnetic.
items.random_wrong: The voltage across the wire creates magnetism.
items.why: Students think magnetism is intrinsic to the wire material rather than a consequence of charge motion.

items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: What happens to the current as it goes around a series circuit?
items.correct: It stays the same at every point — current is conserved.
items.misconception: Current gets used up by each component as it flows around the circuit.
items.random_wrong: Current splits in half at the battery so each direction gets some.
items.why: Students confuse current (charge flow) with energy (which is transferred), thinking the bulb 'uses up' current rather than energy.

items.q: Split-ring commutator in a DC motor:
items.why: Correct answer: reverses current every half turn. “increases voltage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: electrical to sound. “sound to electrical” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Doubling field strength while keeping I,L same multiplies force by:
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
